title: Why 'Let's Think Step by Step' Works
concept: Forcing intermediate reasoning tokens gives the model more compute per answer, revisited from a prompt-craft lens.

exercises.prompt: As a recap, what is the core mechanism LLMs use to generate text?

options.text: Predicting the next token based on previous text.
Querying a relational database using SQL.
Compiling source code into machine language.

exercises.explain: In earlier lessons, we learned that LLMs predict the next token based on context. This prompt engineering technique builds on that concept by adding reasoning to the context.

cards.title: The Magic Words
cards.body: We've all heard the magic phrase 'Let's think step by step.' But have you ever wondered why these five words dramatically improve an LLM's accuracy? It's not magic—it's basic computer science!

cards.title: Intermediate Tokens as Compute
cards.body: When you prompt an LLM to 'think step by step', you force it to generate intermediate reasoning tokens. Because an LLM does a fixed amount of math for every token it outputs, more tokens mean more total computation time dedicated to solving your problem before it commits to a final answer.

cards.title: The Scratchpad Effect
cards.body: Think of an LLM as a mental math wizard who isn't allowed to use scratch paper. If you ask them to multiply two big numbers instantly, they will likely guess wrong. But if you give them scratch paper to write down each step, they can easily solve it!

cards.title: Breaking Down the Answer
cards.body: Instead of asking 'Who won the 1998 World Cup and what is their capital city?' and getting an immediate incorrect guess, you ask the model to break it down. The model writes out the winner (France) first, and then uses that generated token to easily retrieve the capital (Paris).

cards.title: No Silent Thinking
cards.body: Many believe the model 'thinks silently' before writing. In reality, LLMs cannot plan ahead in their 'heads'; they can only calculate the very next word. The reasoning must happen live, right on the screen.

cards.title: Direct vs. Step-by-Step
cards.body: Comparing how an LLM handles a hard question with and without intermediate reasoning tokens.

left.text: Tries to guess the final answer immediately. High chance of error.

right.text: Generates intermediate tokens, building a path to the correct answer.

exercises.prompt: Why does generating intermediate steps help an LLM solve math problems?

options.text: The model searches the internet while it writes the steps.
The model gets to perform computation for each intermediate token it generates.
The prompt changes the model's underlying neural network structure.
It makes the model run on a faster processor.

exercises.explain: LLMs generate text one token at a time. If you force an immediate answer, the model only has a single token's worth of computation to get it right. Letting it write steps gives it more computational steps.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An LLM cannot perform multi-step planning silently in its 'head' before outputting a token.
exercises.explain: Because LLMs predict token-by-token, they cannot think ahead. If they don't print their intermediate steps, they must guess the final answer using only one step of calculation.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe what intermediate tokens provide to the model:
exercises.sentence: Generating intermediate reasoning tokens gives the model more compute to solve complex tasks.
exercises.explain: Each token generated requires a forward pass of the neural network. More tokens generated means more compute time applied to the problem.

exercises.prompt: Identify which response demonstrates the use of intermediate reasoning tokens to calculate 11 * 12:

options.text: First, multiply 11 by 10 to get 110. Then, multiply 11 by 2 to get 22. Finally, add 110 and 22 to get 132.
I think the result of 11 times 12 is probably around 130.

exercises.explain: The second option shows the AI breaking down the calculation step-by-step before arriving at the final answer, illustrating Chain-of-Thought reasoning.

exercises.prompt: From an engineering perspective, why does forcing the model to write out its reasoning steps before the final answer actually improve accuracy?

options.text: It triggers a hidden database inside the model that stores pre-calculated answers.
It converts a hard single-step prediction into a sequence of easier next-token predictions, leveraging more total compute.
It forces the model to run its training process in reverse to find the correct weights.
It bypasses the transformer architecture entirely to run raw python code.

exercises.explain: Intermediate tokens act as an external working memory. Because LLMs compute next tokens sequentially, generating these step-by-step reasoning steps allows the model to process complex calculations and logical transitions that are impossible to resolve in a single, direct output step.
